speaker: interviewer
text: 30 seconds of bars, and then we'll be ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's talk about how, through your work in the photography studio, you got involved with the underground movement.

speaker: interviewee
text: Alexandra? Well-- excuse  me.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this, exactly?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was early '41-- late-- late 1940. The winter of 1940. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] One morning, in the middle of the winter, this young woman came into the studio. I was-- I'd just opened the studio, very early in the morning. And-- I remember the door was very loose, and she let it go. And it made a lot of rocket noise, and the windowpane started shaking. And she sort of-- I looked up, and I saw this young woman. Very apologetically, she said, oh, she said, god, I'm sorry. I closed the door too loud. And eventually, she came in. And she said-- I told her not to worry about it, that everything shakes in this place, something like that. Everything is loose, and everything is shaking.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she said that she would like to take a picture for  her ID card, for an ID. Could she have it the following day? And I said, yes, of course. And I looked at her, and I-- my knees were buckling, started buckling under me. I mean, I'd never seen anything so beautiful before-- as long as I lived, really. There was something magnificent about her, her face. It was just great. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Sorry. [LAUGHS] Just, I-- well, sorry you had to talk about these things, really. Anyway, and she-- I took a-- I took a picture. I took a picture. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and the following day, she came in, again, to pick them up. They were ready. And-- and she-- I remember her commenting that these pictures are good enough to simply give them away to my relatives. She said she would probably want to blow some of them up, blow up, and make a few enlargements. Because these were little ID cards, something like two by three, no more than that, very small. And I said, sure, they could be blown up. And eventually, we-- make a long story short, she came back, again, the following week, with some pictures, with some old pictures to be copied. She needed some copies  of some old pictures, some family pictures or something like that. And slowly and surely, she became a steady guest. I would say at least once a week or so, she would be in. And of  course, she attracted a lot of attention. I remember, Mr. Orenstein would make comments, occasionally. And one day she came in with her little nice, [? Kasha, ?] a beautiful, little blonde kid. And I took a picture of the two of them. And I guess we-- I remember, Mr. Orenstein got very mad at the fact that I spent so much time taking that picture, because there was a long line of people to be photographed, waiting in the office. And I took too much time. And he really let me know about it. That--

speaker: interviewer
text: And eventually, the-- we-- after a number of months, she simply came out, one day, to tell-- to ask me whether-- she more or less let me know what she would be interested in, that she would be interested in photographs that the Nazis dropped off sometimes. Could she talk to me about these things?

speaker: interviewee
text: But this is very dangerous. [INAUDIBLE] She-- she was definitely question which is-- could be very dangerous. She said that first, I would have I would have to promise her that it's between us, that this is not gonna go out between us. And I said, sure, I do promise. So we shook hands.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she said, look, I'm interested in photographs of Nazi atrocities. I know Germans photograph these things, and they drop a film to be processed. They don't process their own film. She says, do you-- are you aware of it? I said, yes, I am. Have you seen such pictures? I said, yes, I see them every day. Do you think you could make a extra copy of some of those real bad, bad, bad stuff, executions and things of that sort? [SIGHS] I remember one thing, that I felt my heart was up in shoulder. I was really-- in-- in one way, I was sort of fascinated by this whole offer. On the other hand, I was absolutely scared. I was scared. I cannot tell you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I looked at her, and I thought to myself, I'll do anything she-- she wants me to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Alexandra.

speaker: interviewer
text: Her last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jylecka. And this is how we developed a liaison. And I want to tell you, there were moments where I-- [SIGHS] I would lift those photos-- those pictures, make copies, and hide them under a box. There was a big closet, a storage closet in the studio, and full of boxes of negatives, old negatives. And I would find a place, under one of those very heavy boxes and keep them in an envelope there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And at some point, I mean-- I mean it was getting too bulky. And I really wondered whether-- I had sleepless nights. All I needed was someone to discover these pictures, specifically of atrocities, right? And right away, you leave. It leads you somewhere, right? And I just had no idea what to do with them. And I was absolutely beside myself. She would not-- there were times when I wouldn't see her more than maybe once a month or so. She would be away a lot, on the road. And in the meantime, I didn't know what to do with those. I was accumulating them, right? So I was afraid to spread them out. I was afraid to take them home. I wouldn't want to  keep them at home, because we had a German family living with us, sharing the same apartment, a couple with a little 10-year-old boy, and-- the [? Terushkas ?].

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I really had a dilemma. Eventually, I found a way to deal with it. In the darkroom, there were two loose boards in the floor, on the right, directly underneath the tank, the-- the chemical tank. Everything was rotting, that floor. And I thought, maybe I'll find a metal box and keep them there, which I did for a while. But then I was afraid that it may leak, the chemicals may leak into it. I had such-- such problems, one after another. I could not simply accumulate those pictures. I wanted to get them out as fast as I could. One day, I didn't see Alexandra for quite a while. And I decided to drop those pictures off to her sister, Joanna's house, which was a few blocks from the studio. They were all involved in the underground, the resistance, sister, her brother-in-law, her brother, Lech, who was supposed to, at some point, rescue me from the other studio, which-- it failed. And one day, I remember, I came, with-- in the middle of the day, without my armband.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was already the ghetto time. You were not allowed to move by yourself in the street. I took off the armband. Do you  realize what kind of a chance I took? I mean, I didn't exactly look Aryan, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was not afraid of the Nazis as much as of the Poles. A Pole would recognize a Jew a mile away. The Nazis did not realize, unless you  had a beard and payots and wore Hasidic garb. But to the Nazis, to be Germans, they couldn't tell a Jew from any-- from a cow. You know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I just ventured to go into Joanna's house with quite a collection of pictures. And I said, look, I must drop them off. She was absolutely-- very annoyed over the fact that I came in without any warning, that this is so dangerous. I shouldn't do this again. And she-- she took the pictures, of course.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said to here, I couldn't keep those pictures any longer, because I didn't know what happened  to Alexandra. She didn't show up, now, in such a long time. And I had to get rid of them, because I have nowhere to-- to hide them. She says, OK, but don't do that again without any warning. This is-- You don't realize how dangerous this is. Maybe somebody watched you coming in here. You never know, she said. You must realize that once you get involved in this movement, you are being watched. Something, someone is watching you all the time. And this really scared me even more [LAUGHS] after-- after she told me this. And eventually, it all worked out well. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know how large the movement was?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had no idea. No information. No one gave you any information, whatsoever. This was a part-- this was a subsidiary of the Polish resistance, Lublin-oriented, the Lublin government, the Lublin-oriented government, OK? This all I knew. This was not the London-oriented government. This was the Lublin-oriented government, which was the left, OK? That all, I knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: But as to who was doing what, who they were, how many people? You never got such information. This is out. The fact is, less you knew, the better off you were. In that sense, the better off everybody else was in case of a downfall, in case of arrest or something. If you knew a lot, chances are, under torture, you started to leak information. That happened, you know. And once you leak information, then everybody goes, right? The less you know, safer it is for the movement. And this was the policy.

speaker: interviewee
text: I just knew Alexandra, Joanna, and her brother, Lech. And that was it-- and [? Victor, ?] later on. [? Victor, ?] who, when I already worked at the other studio, in '42, '43, and one day [? Victor ?] come-- come comes along to tell me that he needs two photos of mine for a-- for a forged ID card. And he says, my name is [? Victor. ?] [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And the idea for that was to help you escape?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And he came to tell me that I shouldn't worry about Alexandra, she's OK. And that his name is [? Victor. ?] And he needs two photos. The following day, he came. I gave him the two photos. And two days later, he came back with the ID, with a-- with my forged ID card.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Branislav ?] [? Gotlewski, ?] the initials didn't change, B-- BG. He-- he-- he said to me, I'll see to it that you'll-- you'll-- you keep-- you'll keep the same initials. And that was the only time I saw him. And I remember him asking me, when I gave him those pictures-- when he-- when he came with the ID card, he said to me, is there any-- anything else that  I can do for you? And I told him, well, I don't-- I can't think of anything. I said, I just lost my mother. She was taken in a deportation. And he says to me, I lost my mother when I was six years old. And he squeezed my shoulder. And he says, be strong.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened? How did the escape attempt fail?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This was late '43. This was in the fall of 1943. One day, Alexandra showed up. This was the second studio, this [? Gniezno ?] studio where I worked.

speaker: interviewer
text: After the Orenstein studio?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the Orenstein, of  course. The Orenstein studio gets confiscated in late summer of '42, after their deportation. And of course, immediately after I get involved with this other studio, and I see Alexandra, occasionally. She would stop in, occasionally, to see me. Very rarely, but not as rare-- as often as she did at the Orenstein studio. And I remember one day, she brought me a sweater and a scarf that she knitted for me. And I remember the sweater and the scarf, I left in Majdanek, when I came in, when they brought me into Majdanek. I hated to leave it, but I had no choice. And one day, she said to me, look, next week, Monday-- I think it was on a Monday. It was going to be on a Monday. Lech, my brother, is going to come here in a Model-T Ford. The back, the window of that workroom, where I was retouching negatives, was facing a gate, an outlet. The studio was located in a corner building, at an intersection, [NON-ENGLISH] Main Street and a side street, right across from a courthouse. And the side street had a gate, where you could, let's say, drive in or enter with a vehicle or whatever, right, into the courtyard. And that window, at which I sat, was facing that gate. So that, from that window, I could see when Lech would arrive. He would-- he would park the car in front of that gate, so that I could see it from the window. And as soon as I see-- as soon as I saw the car, I was told, slowly, take your time, don't rush, don't carry anything with you, don't take anything. Just take your coat. Have your toothbrush. Don't take anything, whatsoever, no suitcases, no boxes, no packages. And walk through the courtyard into the car. Somebody will open the car for you from inside. You hop in, and you're gone. Because I worked with the other two people, colleagues, Jews at the studio, and the problem was that, as soon as I noticed-- of course, that day I was totally restless, because I knew that was-- this was it. This was the day I was going to be rescued.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, you can imagine the anticipation, anxiety, all that. It really worked on me. And then all the rumors about liquidation of the ghetto were already known. And we didn't know how long it's gonna be, how long-- how much longer are we going to be around. What are they going to do with us? What else is left? What options are there?

speaker: interviewee
text: And sure enough, around 5 o'clock in the afternoon, it was already getting dark, after dusk. I noticed a car. And the problem was that I reacted too rapidly. As soon as I noticed it, I just got up. And there were other people in this workroom. And they noticed it, probably. They must have noticed that this was not the usual way I get up from a chair. I sort of did it a little-- with too much impetus. And I walked out too fast. And sure enough, on the way out, I just grabbed my coat. And my hat was in my pocket, I remember. And there was a back door, out into the courtyard, through which I was going to take it to the car. And as soon as I reached the back door, I had to open it. There was a chain on it. As soon as I touched the chain, I heard this-- one of my colleagues, a young boy, scream, call the boss, the owner of the studio. Mr. [? Skenecia ?], Bernard is escaping.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that a Jewish boy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. Yes. It didn't take more than two seconds. He was right on top of me. He knocked me down. He-- he sat on top of me. And he was quite a bulky guy, [? Skenecia, ?] the owner. And he was going to call the-- the Gestapo. Hah! He was going to call the Gestapo. And you know what would happen then? It would be out.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said to him, look, I wasn't going anywhere. I was going to go out to get some air. What is he-- you know? What is he-- But why did you take your coat? I said, because it's cold outside. I said, why does he carry on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, and he sat on top of me practically until the policeman came, from the ghetto, to pick us up, to escort us back to-- to the ghetto, which happened almost like 45 minutes later. He sat on top of me. I mean, I thought I'll suffocate, really. I had to beg him, please, move off my chest, because I can't breathe anymore. Anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why did he scream? Because he was afraid that, if they will go back two instead of three of us, that they will hold them responsible for the fact that an [INAUDIBLE]-- To the ghetto that nigh?

speaker: interviewee
text: --escaped. I didn't think this was the case. They wouldn't hold responsible anybody for anything, for the simple reason that they couldn't care less. The ghetto was controlled by the Jewish cops. There were no-- I mean the Germans hardly paid attention at that time. Except that-- unless there was something going to happen, like a deportation, a liquidation, a round-up. So this is when the Germans would-- would come in and staff their own people and put guards around it. But, at that point, there was nobody there, really, as far as I know, as far as I could tell. But still, he wanted to play it safe. He didn't want to take a risk. Therefore, he risked my life instead of taking a chance. So this is how they foiled my escape. And Lech stood-- stood there for the longest time, until he left or something went wrong, and he took off. Good for him that he took off. You see, I could have still, if I really had more guts, at the time, I-- when he started to scream, I could've run out-- because there wasn't much of a distance to the car-- get into the car and take off. Except I was afraid that they might sound an alarm, alert the Nazis. And they may pursue the car. And he would have been in some trouble. And I didn't want to do it. I mean, this would jeopardy-- jeopardize somebody else's life. And I thought of it, just a split of a second, it occurred to me, no. I'm not going to take that chance. I'm not going to run. I'm staying. And this is how it happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the following morning, I was on a cattle train, on the way to Majdanek. And what day was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: This had to be a Tuesday or a Wednesday, I know that. I'm not sure exactly. But I guess this is what it was, middle of the week when we arrived, when we were on-- on that train. And on that train, also, was my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother had been taken already.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was taken in '42. Do you remember 'On Guilt,' that story?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, that story, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, right. And on that train--

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was '43, when you were taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: '43. Late '43, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Late '43.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I arrived in Majdanek the following day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, let's talk about how it was when you were deported. Like how much you remember of that whole day.

speaker: interviewee
text: You mean the day I was taken, on the train to-- on the way to Majdanek?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: I do remember quite a bit, I think. I remember-- well, most of the people on the train, I knew. They were all from-- from this-- from Radom, from the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were on the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say there must have been close to 1,000 people, at that time, that they got on that train. They also took some people from the Szkolna camp. And my sister was on that train, too, of which I was not aware, because they kept the women separately. But I did find out, when we arrived in Mauthausen, a few days later. I found out that she was there, too. There were my-- two of my aunts, my sister, and some friends of theirs, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: But what happened actually that day, on the way to Majdanek, is that I met my brother's friend, Leon, in that car. We were in the same car. And of course, he was someone that I knew intimately. I knew him since I was a child. He was my brother's best friend. You remember him from the story, my brother's friend, right? Mm hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we kept very close. And he was-- he was a very interesting man.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the conditions--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was full of  hope

speaker: interviewer
text: --like on the train, on the ride? How long was the train ride?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the train luckily was not very long. We rode about 24 hours, a trip which didn't-- shouldn't take more than three hours. They were always prolonged, these rides, because trains would stop to let other trains through. This was at the height of the Russian-- German-Russian war. And there were a lot of trains coming, going back and forth through Poland, with equipment, to the front, with wounded and people coming from the front, east and west. So every time a train had to go by, our train would pull off to the side and stay there for hours, stand there for hours. Cold, you cannot imagine, freezing. There was no heat. We're talking about cattle trains, cattle cars.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any food, any water?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody gave us any food or anything. You just had-- if you had-- if you took anything with you. What could you have taken? Some bread? That's about all. If you had some bread, you were lucky. They didn't give you anything, whatsoever. This was considered a short ride. I mean, 24 hours, it's nothing, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they let you out of the train at any point?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did people see you, the local population, as the train was passing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course not, nothing. These cars were sealed. And the little windows that they had were wired. No, you couldn't or anything. And the population wasn't so interested in any of that. I mean, they had no interest to keep contact with the deported Jews. You probably remember--

speaker: interviewer
text: But they were aware of what was happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sure they were aware, yes. But the countryside was kind of empty. But you couldn't see very  much. I mean, there were not many people around to begin with. This was wintertime. But nobody worked out in the fields. And so it was desolate, really. The whole-- And we arrived at Mauthausen-- at Majdanek. And, of course, the scene was the same as any arrival. You went through the showers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, what was-- was it day or evening when you   arrived?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was morning, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Morning.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was like early morning when we arrived. And we were brought into the showers. And we didn't know whether they were showers or not. I do remember-- because, at that time, we  had already heard enough stories about the gas chambers and, and all that, the way they fool you with it, bring you into the showers. You think it's going to-- instead of water, you get gas.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the whole registration process?

speaker: interviewee
text: Registration? Very simple. You came-- You went through a line. And you were shaven, and your hair was cut clean. You had no hair left. And they-- You simply followed-- they show you where to go. Eventually, you wound up in-- you left all your clothes off. You were just like naked, in the nude. You went into this huge place, which was the shower room. And I do remember I-- I was able to take, with me, my forged ID card. [LAUGHS] We must have had some clothing when we went into the shower room, because we got undressed in the shower room. This is where we left the clothing on the side of the shower room. And I took out my ID card, took it with me to the shower. And I ripped it up into these tiny, tiny, little pieces. And I let it down, into the drain, with the water. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was the end of the forged identity. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And eventually, after two minutes or so, they run you out, or they run you out of the shower room. And there was another barrack that you had to run through, naked. And some people were throwing some clothing articles at you, which were totally, I mean, out of place and out of size. And most of these things had a lot of paint. They were painted over. If you ever-- if you ever escape from a camp, you could be easily recognized by the red paint on your clothing. You know, oil paint, dried oil paint, stripes on your back or crosses or whatever else, all kinds of forms that they painted. But usually-- it was a-- it was a horrible feeling to wear a jacket. It sort of weighed you down, the dry paint. I mean it was awful. And these are the things you wind up with and a pair of clogs. And luckily I-- and, of course, they run you outside. You are still wet. You know, you must realized, there are no towels to get dry or anything. You come out of the shower, wet, and you have a bundle of those rags in your arms. And then they run you outside in the snow, ice. And this is where you get dressed. This was the point when something happened, something, in my case, something incredible happened. [? Anton ?] shows up. He was a kapo. And that-- He was a kapo in the shower room. And here is this man, I hardly recognized him. I mean--

speaker: interviewer
text: This was someone you knew from Radom?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Well, I mean, I had no idea that he was in Majdanek. He comes out to make sure that it is I. Because, apparently, he noticed me when they were throwing the clothing articles around. And he came over to me when I was getting dressed outside. He says, what are you doing here, boy? This isn't a place for-- for you. And that was all.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then he walked away. And the following day, in the barrack-- we were already assigned to a barrack-- he shows up, flannel shirt, a jacket, socks, underwear, a whole bundle of clothing, and half a loaf of bread. I was totally flabbergasted by the sole generosity of this. I mean, it was more than I ever expected. A jacket, a warm jacket, I mean I looked like-- this was not clothing for a camp, you know? I looked like I was overdressed, really. I was almost embarrassed to wear these things. Of course, nobody else had such things. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- it was really sheer luck to have this happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was-- he had a cruel reputation.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, absolutely. I mean people were afraid of him. Absolutely, they were scared stiff of him.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were in the barracks with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- This barrack I was in could have held about 400 people. It was a huge barrack.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many people were sleeping in the same?

speaker: interviewee
text: Double bunks, in some cases triple-- but-- triple. Well, [? someone-- ?] I do remember one thing. When I came in, I-- I had the upper bunk. And I remember, when I climbed up on the bunk, I saw icicles coming from the rafters, right over my bunk, a little slightly off over. So I chopped them down. And two days later-- there must have been a leak in the roof. And, of course, the moisture froze, and we-- I was getting icicles. So every few days, I would just knock them down. And then later, they would come back again. It was just incredibly cold and nasty. And-- Well, the winters in Poland are notorious for being nasty and very long winters, too. And of course, with the hunger and everything else, there was not much-- nothing, there was not much here, I'll tell you. But I do remember one scene, which sort of stuck in my mind. When we came out of the showers and got dressed and were aligned on that-- on that place, that square, right inside the camp, and when we came out dressed like in those strange uniforms, suddenly, we looked at one another, and we burst out laughing. And I do remember my brother's friend, Leon, was standing right in front of me. And he turned around. We were already in rows of five. He turned around. And he looked at me. And I looked at him. And the two of us just-- I mean, I never laughed so hard in my life. And this happened to everybody else, because the comical clothing that we wore, it was absolutely--

speaker: interviewer
text: Looking at each other.

speaker: interviewee
text: --it [? was ?] like a stage. It was simply a theater, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can we just stop for one second?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
